summary: McKenzie explains how to use CrazyEgg's heatmap tracking on pages that require authentication, which normally blocks CrazyEgg's screenshot bot. The solution involves detecting CrazyEgg's user-agent string and bypassing the login procedure for their bot. He created a dedicated trial account for CrazyEgg's bot to authenticate as, enabling heatmap data collection on logged-in application pages. He acknowledges this approach has security implications but argues it's proportionate to his threat model.
key_points: CrazyEgg's heatmap tracking doesn't work behind login screens by default because the screenshot bot can't authenticate
You can detect CrazyEgg's bot via its user-agent string and bypass authentication for it
For security-sensitive applications, CrazyEgg provides IP addresses to whitelist instead of user-agent detection
The pragmatic solution is to create a dedicated trial account and auto-authenticate the bot as that user
money_quotes: They're seriously my favorite $19 to pay every month, even when I don't actually use them, because some day I know I'll get the itch again and then bam actionable insights into what my customers are doing.
I sell bingo cards to 60 year old women.
In Rails, doing something on the basis of the user agent is easy but, and I know this might come as a surprise, not covered in the documentation.
key_insight: You can extend analytics tools like CrazyEgg behind login screens by detecting their bot's user-agent and auto-authenticating it as a dedicated account, calibrating security tradeoffs to your actual threat model.
